On this page

Peripherals — Input, Output, and Storage Devices ​

The CPU and memory are the brain and short-term memory of a computer. Peripherals are everything else — the devices that let you put information in, get results out, and store data permanently.

Grade 10+

Peripheral devices are introduced in Grade 10 as part of the hardware unit. The classification and examples are core Paper 2 content.

What is a Peripheral? ​

A peripheral is any external device connected to a computer that provides input, output, communication, or storage. The CPU and RAM are not peripherals — everything else typically is.

Categories:

  • Input devices — send data INTO the computer
  • Output devices — display/produce data FROM the computer
  • Storage devices — store data permanently (can be both input and output)
  • Communication devices — connect the computer to networks or other devices

Input Devices ​

An input device converts real-world data into a digital signal the CPU can process.

DeviceTypeHow it worksUse
KeyboardAlphanumericEach key press sends a digital codeTyping text and commands
MousePointingTracks movement; buttons send click signalsNavigate GUI, select items
Trackpad / touchpadPointingCapacitive surface detects finger movementLaptop navigation
TouchscreenPointing + gestureCapacitive or resistive surface detects touch coordinatesSmartphones, tablets, ATMs
Barcode scannerOpticalLaser reads light/dark patterns of a barcodeRetail, inventory, library
QR code readerOpticalCamera decodes 2D matrix patternsMenus, payments, links
Scanner (flatbed)OpticalCCD captures reflected light from a documentDigitise documents and images
Webcam / cameraOpticalCaptures video/image framesVideo calls, photos, surveillance
MicrophoneAudioConverts sound waves to electrical signalVoice input, recording
Digital cameraOpticalCCD/CMOS sensor captures imagePhotography
Joystick / gamepadPointingAnalogue movement + buttonsGaming, flight simulation
Stylus / graphics tabletPointingPressure-sensitive drawing surfaceDigital art, CAD
Biometric readerBiometricFingerprint, iris, face recognitionSecurity authentication
OCR scannerOpticalRecognises printed text and converts to editable textDocument digitisation
MICR readerMagneticReads magnetic ink characters (bottom of cheques)Bank cheque processing
SensorsEnvironmentalTemperature, light, motion — converts physical to digitalIoT, embedded systems

Output Devices ​

An output device receives processed data from the CPU and presents it to the user.

DeviceTypeDetailUse
Monitor (LCD/LED)VisualPixels lit by backlight; LED is energy-efficientPrimary visual output
OLED monitorVisualEach pixel generates its own light; deeper blacksHigh-end displays
ProjectorVisualProjects enlarged image onto a surfacePresentations, cinema
Printer (inkjet)PrintedSprays tiny ink droplets onto paperHome/office documents
Printer (laser)PrintedUses static electricity + toner powderHigh-volume office printing
3D printerPrintedDeposits material layer by layerPrototyping, manufacturing
SpeakersAudioConvert digital audio signal to sound wavesMusic, alerts, voice
HeadphonesAudioPersonal audio outputPrivate listening
PlotterVisualDraws precise lines — pen moves on paperEngineering drawings, CAD
Braille displayTactileRaises/lowers pins to form Braille charactersAccessibility for visually impaired

Inkjet vs Laser Printer ​

FeatureInkjetLaser
TechnologyLiquid ink sprayed onto paperPowdered toner fused by heat
Cost of printerLowerHigher
Cost per pageHigher (ink cartridges expensive)Lower (toner lasts longer)
SpeedSlowerFaster
QualityExcellent for photos, colourExcellent for sharp text
Best forHome use, photos, occasional colourHigh-volume office printing

Storage Devices ​

Storage devices hold data permanently (non-volatile) — data persists when power is off.

DeviceTechnologySpeedCapacityUse
HDD (Hard Disk Drive)Magnetic spinning platters~100–160 MB/sUp to 20 TBCheap bulk storage
SSD (Solid State Drive)Flash memory chips500 MB/s–7 GB/sUp to 4 TB commonFast OS/app storage
USB Flash DriveFlash memoryModerate4 GB–2 TBPortable file transfer
SD Card / microSDFlash memoryVariesUp to 1 TBCameras, phones, tablets
Optical disc (DVD)Laser reads/writesSlow4.7 GB (DVD)Media distribution
Optical disc (Blu-ray)Laser reads/writesSlow25–100 GBHD video, archiving
Magnetic tapeSequential magneticVery slow accessHundreds of TBArchival backup
Cloud storageRemote serversDepends on internetVirtually unlimitedBackup, sync, sharing

Communication Devices ​

These connect computers to networks or other devices.

DeviceFunction
Network Interface Card (NIC)Connects computer to wired (Ethernet) network
Wireless NIC (Wi-Fi card)Connects to wireless networks
ModemConverts digital data to analogue for phone lines (and back)
RouterConnects networks; directs data packets between networks; provides DHCP
SwitchConnects devices on the SAME network; faster than hub
HubConnects devices on same network; broadcasts to all (less efficient than switch)
Wireless Access Point (WAP)Extends wireless network coverage
Bluetooth adapterShort-range wireless connection for peripherals
4G/5G modem/dongleMobile internet connection via cellular network

Ports and Connectors ​

PortUse
USB-AStandard USB; most peripherals
USB-CModern connector; supports data + video + power; reversible
HDMIHigh-definition video and audio output to monitor/TV
DisplayPortVideo output (often used for high-refresh monitors)
Ethernet (RJ-45)Wired network connection
3.5mm audio jackHeadphones/speakers/microphone
SD card slotInsert SD cards directly
ThunderboltVery high speed — data + video + charging in one port

Plug and Play (PnP) ​

Plug and Play is the ability of an operating system to automatically detect and configure a newly connected device — no manual driver installation by the user.

  1. User plugs in device
  2. OS detects new hardware
  3. OS searches for and installs the correct driver
  4. Device is ready to use

Most modern devices (USB peripherals, monitors, printers) are Plug and Play.

Key Terms ​

TermDefinition
PeripheralAny device connected to a computer (not the CPU or RAM)
Input deviceDevice that sends data into the computer
Output deviceDevice that presents processed data from the computer
Storage deviceDevice that permanently holds data
HDDHard Disk Drive — magnetic storage with spinning platters
SSDSolid State Drive — flash memory, no moving parts
OCROptical Character Recognition — scanning printed text to editable text
MICRMagnetic Ink Character Recognition — reads magnetic ink on cheques
ModemConverts digital data to analogue signals for phone line transmission
NICNetwork Interface Card — hardware enabling network connection
WAPWireless Access Point — extends Wi-Fi coverage
Plug and PlayOS automatically detects and configures new hardware

Exam Focus ​

High-Frequency Questions

  1. "Classify each device as input/output/storage and give a reason" — the reason must link to the direction of data flow, not just the device name

  2. "Give ONE example of each: input, output, storage device" — must give correct examples; printer is output, hard drive is storage, keyboard is input

  3. "Compare inkjet and laser printers" — minimum two criteria: cost per page (laser cheaper), speed (laser faster), quality (laser text, inkjet photo)

  4. "What is the difference between a hub and a switch?" — hub broadcasts to all devices; switch sends only to the intended device (more efficient, faster)

  5. "Give an advantage of an SSD over an HDD" — no moving parts, faster, quieter, more shock-resistant, uses less power
